title: ETFs vs. Mutual Funds for Beginners: Which Should You Choose in 2026?
excerpt: Compare ETFs and mutual funds in plain English, including costs, taxes, minimums, and which option makes the most sense for beginner investors.
targetKeyword: etfs vs mutual funds for beginners
relatedKeywords: which is better etf or mutual fund
content:
## Two Popular Funds, One Common Beginner Question

If you are ready to start investing, you will quickly run into two fund types that seem very similar: ETFs and mutual funds. Both let you buy a diversified basket of investments in a single purchase. Both can be low-cost. Both can hold stocks, bonds, or a mix of assets.

So what is the real difference, and which one should a beginner choose?

In most cases, the best answer depends less on which label sounds better and more on how you want to invest. Your account type, starting balance, and preference for automation matter a lot. If you are brand new, this guide will help you make a simple, confident choice without overthinking it.

## What Is an ETF?

ETF stands for exchange-traded fund. An ETF is a fund that trades on an exchange during the day, similar to a stock. When you buy one share of an ETF, you are buying a small piece of a larger portfolio that may contain hundreds or even thousands of investments.

Many ETFs track broad indexes, which makes them a popular choice for beginner investors who want diversification without picking individual stocks. If you need a refresher on index investing, read our guide to [index funds for beginners](/blog/index-funds-for-beginners).

### Key ETF traits

- Trades throughout the day while the market is open
- Usually bought in a brokerage account
- Often has low expense ratios
- Can usually be purchased starting with the price of one share, and some brokers allow fractional shares

## What Is a Mutual Fund?

A mutual fund also pools money from many investors into one portfolio. The biggest difference is how it is bought and sold. Mutual funds are priced once per day after the market closes. Every investor who buys or sells that day gets the same end-of-day net asset value, also called NAV.

Mutual funds can be actively managed or index-based. Many employer retirement plans and automatic investing programs are built around mutual funds, which is one reason they remain extremely common.

### Key mutual fund traits

- Bought directly from the fund company or through a broker
- Priced once per day after markets close
- May have investment minimums
- Often easier to automate with recurring dollar contributions

## ETFs vs. Mutual Funds: The Biggest Differences

Both fund types can be smart choices. The right pick comes down to a few practical differences.

### 1. How they trade

This is the clearest difference. ETFs trade during the day, so prices move up and down while the market is open. Mutual funds settle once each day at the closing NAV.

For long-term investors, this difference usually matters less than beginners expect. If you are investing for retirement or goals that are years away, you do not need minute-by-minute trading. Still, some people like the flexibility of ETFs, especially if they already use a brokerage account.

### 2. Minimum investment

ETFs are often easier to start with because you can usually buy a single share, and many brokerages now offer fractional shares. That lowers the barrier to entry for someone starting with $25, $50, or $100.

Mutual funds sometimes require an initial minimum, though some providers have reduced or eliminated those minimums. If you are still building your first investing habit, lower minimums can make it easier to begin.

If you only have a small amount to start, you may also like our guide on [how to start investing with 100 dollars](/blog/how-to-start-investing-with-100-dollars).

### 3. Automation and recurring investing

Mutual funds often have a small edge here. Many brokerages make it very easy to invest an exact dollar amount every week or month into a mutual fund. That can be ideal if you want a set-it-and-forget-it system.

ETFs are catching up, and some brokers now support recurring ETF purchases too. Still, the experience can vary by platform. If your top priority is full automation, mutual funds may feel simpler.

### 4. Taxes in taxable accounts

According to Investor.gov, ETFs have historically been more tax-efficient than many mutual funds because they typically distribute fewer capital gains. That matters most in a regular taxable brokerage account.

In tax-advantaged accounts such as IRAs and 401(k)s, this difference is much less important. Investor.gov specifically notes there is no tax difference between an ETF and a mutual fund when held in a tax-advantaged account. So if you are investing inside a Roth IRA, focus more on cost, simplicity, and available fund choices.

If you are deciding where to invest, our [Roth IRA beginner's guide](/blog/what-is-roth-ira-beginners-guide) can help.

### 5. Fees and costs

ETFs are often known for low costs, but the fund structure alone does not guarantee a bargain. Some mutual funds are also very cheap, especially index mutual funds from major providers.

When comparing two funds, look at:

- Expense ratio
- Trading commissions, if any
- Account fees
- Bid-ask spread for ETFs
- Sales loads or transaction fees for some mutual funds

For most beginners, the best move is to choose a low-cost broad-market fund and keep contributing consistently. A small difference in fees matters, but not as much as getting started and staying invested.

### 6. Intraday pricing and order control

ETFs give you more control over how you place an order. You can use market orders, limit orders, and other trading tools. That flexibility is useful for experienced investors, but it is not essential for most beginners.

Mutual funds are simpler. You invest a dollar amount, and the order fills at the end of the day. For many people, that simplicity is a benefit, not a drawback.

## Similarities Beginners Should Not Ignore

The differences get most of the attention, but the similarities are just as important. Investor.gov notes that both ETFs and mutual funds can offer diversification, professional management, liquidity, and either active or passive strategies.

That means you do not need to frame this as a life-changing decision. In many cases, a low-cost ETF and a low-cost index mutual fund that track the same market will perform very similarly over the long run.

The bigger wins usually come from:

- Starting early
- Contributing regularly
- Staying diversified
- Keeping costs low
- Avoiding panic selling

## Which Is Better for Beginners?

There is no universal winner, but there is a practical winner for your situation.

### An ETF may be better if you:

- Are opening a regular brokerage account
- Want to start with a small amount
- Prefer broad low-cost index investing
- Like the flexibility of buying during market hours
- Have access to fractional share investing

### A mutual fund may be better if you:

- Want easy automatic monthly investing
- Prefer investing exact dollar amounts
- Are using a workplace retirement plan or provider built around mutual funds
- Want the simplest possible hands-off system

### For many beginners, both are good enough

That is the honest answer. If you choose a diversified, low-cost fund and keep investing, you are probably making a smart move either way. The worst choice is usually staying in cash because you feel stuck choosing between two solid options.

## What About Index Funds?

This is where beginners often get confused. "Index fund" is not the opposite of ETF. An index fund describes how a fund invests, not how it is packaged.

You can have:

- An index ETF
- An index mutual fund
- An actively managed ETF
- An actively managed mutual fund

So the better comparison is often index ETF vs. index mutual fund, not ETF vs. index fund. If your goal is simple long-term growth, either version can work very well.

## A Simple Decision Framework

If you are stuck, use this quick framework:

### Choose an ETF if:

1. You are using a brokerage account.
2. You want low minimums.
3. You are comfortable buying shares.

### Choose a mutual fund if:

1. You want automatic recurring contributions.
2. You prefer exact dollar investing.
3. Your provider offers excellent low-cost mutual funds with no extra fees.

### Choose whichever low-cost option is easier to stick with

Consistency matters more than fund packaging. A simple habit of monthly investing will likely do more for your net worth than squeezing out tiny structural advantages.

Dollar-cost averaging can help make that habit easier, especially if market swings make you nervous. We explain that approach in our guide to [dollar-cost averaging for beginners](/blog/what-is-dollar-cost-averaging-beginners-guide).

## Common Beginner Mistakes to Avoid

### Chasing trendy funds

Some ETFs and mutual funds are narrowly focused on one sector, theme, or strategy. These can be much riskier than broad market funds. Beginners are usually better off starting with total market or S&P 500 style funds.

### Ignoring the expense ratio

A fund that sounds exciting can still be expensive. Over decades, higher fees quietly eat into returns. Compare costs before you invest.

### Leaving cash uninvested

Opening an account is only half the job. You also need to actually buy the fund. Many beginners deposit money and forget this final step.

### Overtrading

Because ETFs trade like stocks, some people feel tempted to watch prices all day. That behavior usually hurts more than it helps. Long-term investing works best when you stay patient.

## Best Accounts for ETFs and Mutual Funds

You can hold either fund type in several account types:

- Taxable brokerage account
- Roth IRA
- Traditional IRA
- 401(k) or similar employer plan

If you are opening your first account, start by understanding your options. Our guide on [how to open a brokerage account](/blog/how-to-open-brokerage-account-beginners-guide) covers the basics.

For retirement investing, a Roth IRA is often a strong starting point because qualified withdrawals are tax-free. Inside that account, choosing between an ETF and a mutual fund is mostly about convenience and available choices.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Are ETFs safer than mutual funds?

Not necessarily. Risk depends more on what the fund owns than whether it is an ETF or mutual fund. A broad stock market ETF and a broad stock market mutual fund can have very similar risk.

### Do ETFs always have lower fees?

No. Many ETFs are cheap, but some mutual funds are also very low-cost. Always compare the actual expense ratio and any account or transaction fees.

### Which is better for retirement, ETFs or mutual funds?

Either can work well for retirement. In a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA or 401(k), taxes are less of a deciding factor, so cost, automation, and fund availability usually matter more.

### Should beginners buy ETFs or mutual funds first?

Beginners should usually choose the low-cost diversified option that is easiest to understand and keep buying consistently. For some people that will be an ETF. For others it will be a mutual fund.

## Sources

- [Investor.gov: Characteristics of Mutual Funds and Exchange-Traded Funds](https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/general-resources/news-alerts/alerts-bulletins/characteristics-mutual-funds-exchange-traded-funds)
- [Investor.gov: Updated Investor Bulletin on ETFs](https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/general-resources/news-alerts/alerts-bulletins/investor-bulletins-24)
- [Vanguard: ETFs vs. Mutual Funds](https://investor.vanguard.com/investor-resources-education/etfs/etf-vs-mutual-fund)

## The Bottom Line

ETFs and mutual funds are both strong tools for beginner investors. The better choice usually comes down to how you invest, how much you are starting with, and how much automation you want.

If you want a simple rule, choose a low-cost diversified fund, automate your contributions if you can, and keep going. Picking between two good fund structures matters far less than building the habit of investing for the long term.
